cases.query: Which sorting algorithm places the largest element in its final position after one pass, and how do the other two algorithms differ in their element movement?

primaryChunks.heading: Mechanism
primaryChunks.text: Each pass scans left-to-right and swaps `a[i]` with `a[i+1]` when they are out of order. After one full pass, the largest element is in its final position. With an early-exit flag, the algorithm stops as soon as a pass makes zero swaps — giving O(n) best case on already-sorted input.  ```mermaid graph TD   A[Start arra…

supportingChunks.heading: Mechanism
supportingChunks.text: Iterate left-to-right. For each element at index `j` (the "key"), shift all larger elements in the sorted prefix one position right, then insert the key into the gap. The sorted prefix grows by one element per iteration.  ```mermaid graph TD   A[Start array A] --> B[Set j to 1]   B --> C{j less than n}   C -->|No| Z[Do…

supportingChunks.heading: Mechanism
supportingChunks.text: For each position `i`, scan `a[i..n-1]` to find the minimum, then swap it with `a[i]`. primaryChunks.heading: Shared Mutable State Between Tests
primaryChunks.text: **What goes wrong**: tests pass individually but fail when run together because one test mutates a static field or shared object that another test reads.  **Why it happens**: static helpers, singleton services, or shared test fixtures with mutable state.  **Mitigation**: create fresh instances in each test's Arrange st…

primaryChunks.text: **What goes wrong**: a "unit" test hits a real database or filesystem, making the suite take minutes instead of seconds.  **Why it happens**: dependencies are not injected — the class creates its own `SqlConnection` or `HttpClient` internally.  **Mitigation**: inject all I/O dependencies as interfaces.
